skill.name: screen-reader-testing
skill.description: Guides screen reader testing for web applications using VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, TalkBack, and Narrator workflows.
skill.summary: Validate web accessibility with practical screen reader testing checklists, commands, and issue patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are markdown examples, keyboard shortcut references, ARIA attributes, and documentation links. No evidence found of executable shell commands, credential access, weak cryptography, data exfiltration, or prompt injection in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Static external command detections are markdown examples
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution detections map to fenced markdown blocks that document screen reader shortcuts, testing checklists, HTML snippets, and JavaScript examples. They are not instructions that execute shell commands or run untrusted input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown code fences or illustrative client-side snippets. No shell interpreter, subprocess call, or executable skill script is present.

low_findings.title: Credential and weak cryptography detections are terminology collisions
low_findings.description: The certificate/key and weak cryptography detections match words such as key, aria-describedby, description, and keyboard navigation examples. These lines describe accessibility behavior and do not contain certificates, private keys, hashes, encryption, or cryptographic APIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows accessibility labels, keyboard event handling, and descriptive text only. No sensitive material or cryptographic implementation exists in the file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation resources
low_findings.description: The four hardcoded URLs are resource links to official screen reader documentation and WebAIM survey material. The skill does not fetch these URLs or transmit data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in a resources list at the end of the markdown file. There is no network client code or automatic request behavior.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance detections are accessibility test steps
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance hits refer to browser, platform, focus, and form testing instructions. They do not collect host information, enumerate files, or inspect system configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is ordinary screen reader testing guidance. It has no commands for host discovery, network scanning, or environment enumeration.

content.value_statement: Screen reader testing is hard to do consistently across platforms and assistive technologies. This skill gives practical commands, checklists, and examples for testing real web interfaces.
actual_capabilities: Explains setup and core commands for VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, TalkBack, and Narrator.
Provides checklists for page load, landmarks, headings, forms, links, buttons, dynamic content, and tables.
Guides testing of modal dialogs, live regions, progress updates, and tab interfaces.
Shows accessible HTML and JavaScript patterns for common screen reader issues.
Offers debugging guidance for accessible names, roles, states, and visibility.
limitations: Does not run automated tests or control a screen reader directly.
Requires access to the target browsers, operating systems, and assistive technologies.
Does not replace testing with disabled users or a full WCAG audit.
Some commands and browser pairings may change as screen readers update.

use_cases.title: Audit a Web Form
use_cases.description: Use structured screen reader steps to verify labels, required states, errors, and focus movement.

use_cases.title: Debug ARIA Widgets
use_cases.description: Check modal, live region, progress, and tab behavior against expected screen reader announcements.

use_cases.title: Plan Accessibility Coverage
use_cases.description: Select practical browser and screen reader combinations for minimum or comprehensive test coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Test Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a screen reader test plan for this web page. Include VoiceOver and NVDA coverage, key workflows, and expected announcements.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me test this form with a screen reader. Focus on labels, required fields, validation errors, instructions, and focus after submit.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Dynamic Updates
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced live region and modal debugging
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this dynamic interface for screen reader issues. Check modal focus, live region announcements, loading states, and return focus behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Assistive Technology Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a cross-screen-reader validation matrix for this workflow. Compare VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, TalkBack, and Narrator expectations.

output_examples.input: Test a checkout form with NVDA and VoiceOver.
output: A step-by-step test script for initial page load, form navigation, invalid submission, error review, and recovery.
Expected announcements for labels, required fields, error text, buttons, and focus changes.
A short list of likely defects and suggested accessibility fixes.

output_examples.input: Review a modal dialog for screen reader support.
output: A checklist covering dialog role, accessible name, description, initial focus, focus trap, Escape behavior, and return focus.
Guidance for testing with keyboard navigation and screen reader browse or focus modes.
Recommended fixes for missing labels, weak focus management, and unannounced state changes.

best_practices: Test with real screen readers after keyboard-only testing passes.
Use semantic HTML first, then add ARIA only when it improves the experience.
Record expected and actual announcements for each critical user workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on one screen reader as proof of complete support.
Do not test only the happy path while skipping errors and loading states.
Do not add ARIA roles without validating focus behavior and announcements.

faq.question: Which screen readers does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, TalkBack, and Narrator, with browser and platform guidance.

faq.question: Does this skill automate screen reader testing?
faq.answer: No. It provides manual test workflows, checklists, expected behavior, and debugging guidance.

faq.question: Can this replace a WCAG audit?
faq.answer: No. It supports assistive technology validation, but a full audit needs broader criteria and evidence.

faq.question: What should I test first?
faq.answer: Start with keyboard access, page title, landmarks, headings, forms, buttons, links, and key user flows.

faq.question: Why test more than one screen reader?
faq.answer: Screen readers and browsers expose content differently, so one passing result may not cover all users.

faq.question: Is this useful for mobile accessibility?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes TalkBack and VoiceOver mobile concepts, but real device testing is still required.
